items.prompt: In this chapter’s usage, reproducibility primarily means:
choices: Same dataset and computational methods yield the same results
Only publishing a journal abstract without data or code
Always collecting an entirely new dataset with a new team
Only storing lineage catalogs across cloud warehouses
items.explain: Reproducibility is obtaining the same results from the same data with the same methods; replicability uses new data or independent conditions.

items.prompt: Replicability tests whether findings hold under new data or independent conditions, not only whether the original computational path can be rerun.
items.explain: Example 13.2 frames replicability as checking generalizability with new climate data and a different team.

items.prompt: Which trio does the chapter treat as core open-science principles?
choices: Only sharding, CAP, and eventual consistency
Only GAN training, mode collapse, and FID
Only HIPAA, GDPR, and differential privacy alone
items.explain: Section 13.1.2 presents accessibility, transparency, and collaboration as the core principles.

items.prompt: Name one platform example used in the chapter for sharing datasets, code, or preprints.
items.rubric: Accept OSF / Open Science Framework, Zenodo, or equivalent named open repository from the part.

items.prompt: Publishing analysis scripts or notebooks is presented as a transparency practice that helps others follow the exact workflow.
items.explain: Example 13.4 covers releasing scripts and notebooks so others can follow the workflow.
